INVESTMENT MANAGERS have cut their position in Europe’s benchmark natural gas futures and options contract to the lowest since before the war between the United States and Iran started. Fund managers sold the equivalent of 51 terawatt-hours in the Dutch TTF contract over the seven days ending on June 19, the fastest sales for 15 months. Funds have sold in nine of the most recent twelve weeks, reducing their position by a total of 139 TWh since March 27. The remaining position has been cut to 184 TWh from 323 TWh at the end of March as traders become more confident about refilling storage ahead of next winter as the Strait of Hormuz re-opens:
U.K. GRID controller warns of insufficient capacity reserve margin during heatwave and warns that it may order maximum generation: https://t.co/m9qWOCFNEZ

“But the oil math was always about inbound tankers more than outbound tankers. Without a steady stream of inbound VLCCs, oil production shut-in continues, eating away at the inherent deficit in the global oil balance. It doesn’t matter whether China is reducing imports or refinery runs; the molecule isn’t replaced.

And so far, the inflow is a trickle. No surprise, because the IRGC is firmly aware of the math. During the 60-day negotiation period, they will max out this leverage and throttle the flow under the disguise of granting permits. By throttling permits, they can effectively control who comes in and who goes out. So you would be a fool if you think they don’t know that controlling the inflow of tankers will ultimately decide how much production shut-in returns.

In addition to the logic I explained above, it is now evident, based on discussions with tanker owners, that the tanker rates offered for ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz are astronomical compared with other regions. Not only is the price insanely high, but operators are having trouble assembling crews willing to take the risk of going in.

So no, the issue is far from resolved, yet everyone still thinks it will somehow be resolved because both sides signed an MOU. Just wait until Trump finds out that IRGC is throttling flows and not actually de-mining the Strait of Hormuz. Will he just ignore this reality and play along or will he have to face the inevitable choice of escalation?

I don’t know, and I don’t really care. What I do care is the number of tankers going in, and right now, the market is going to be very wrong about this because the IRGC is going to throttle flows.”

#Commodities:

The Bloomberg Commodity Total Return Index is heading for its worst monthly performance since 2022, currently down around 10% in June and reducing its year-to-date gain to 15%. All sectors are trading lower this month, led by a 16% slump in energy and a 12% decline in precious metals. Industrial metals and agriculture are both down around 6%, highlighting the broad-based nature of the latest commodity market correction, with the main drivers being the US-Iran peace deal, a hawkish Fed, and a stronger dollar.

Gold fell for a second session, pressured by a stronger dollar amid a technology-led equity selloff. Limited support came from rising Treasury bonds as lower energy prices ease concerns about inflation and reduce the need for additional Fed tightening. Instead, gold's unusually strong positive correlation with the S&P 500 continues to weigh on prices, pushing the metal into the key USD 4,000–4,100 support zone. A break below could trigger a fresh wave of capitulation and technical selling. Watch US equities, the dollar, and silver for direction, with the latter now challenging its March low near USD 61.

Brent crude slipped below USD 77 per barrel, leaving prices less than 10% above pre-war levels, as tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz continues to normalise. The International Maritime Organization said it has received safety assurances supporting the passage of hundreds of vessels out of the Persian Gulf. The market is increasingly pricing a steady return of stranded supply following one of the most severe disruptions in recent history. UAE exports have already recovered to around 85% of pre-war levels, adding to expectations of improving supply availability in the weeks ahead.

Cocoa retreated from a six-week high after Monday's 10% surge, as rising El Niño-related production concerns were offset by favourable near-term weather forecasts in West Africa. Improved rainfall across key growing regions has boosted crop development prospects, highlighting the ongoing tug-of-war between longer-term weather risks and near-term supply conditions.

In a report released on Monday, the investment bank outlined that EV car        sales in the market increased by 3.4 percentage points in May. EVs        accounted for 26.1% of total sales in the global market.
The study outlined demand for oil in the global market could take a hit        of around 130,000 barrels/day by December next year if there is no        growth in EV sales.
However, under the “Persistent ⁠Acceleration” scenario, oil demand could        be offset by 320,000 barrels/day during the same period if the growth        follows the February to May path.

WTI crude oil taps early-March lows near $71/bbl. The shipping recovery in the Strait of Hormuz and slowing global demand are crushing any rebound momentum. 

🟢 Goldman Sachs (The Moderate):
Expects WTI to target ~$75 by Q4, with a full-year average range of $70–$78. They see high-level volatility persisting as geopolitical wildcards linger.

🔴 JPMorgan (The Bear):
Sticking firmly to its bearish outlook, forecasting a major slide down to $55–$58.

What Oil/LNP disruption? Goldman says one factor will send crude prices plunging in the coming year. Goldman Sachs says rising EV adoption will push the price of oil down through 2027.
Tensions around the Strait of Hormuz could still keep prices in flux, but Goldman says they're pointed down. Two factors - the Iran War and EV sales.
EV sales are surging in the aftermath of the Iran war, the analyst say.
The oil shock that boosted crude prices to their highest level in years could soon be a distant memory.

Goldman Sachs says that as the Iran war seems to be winding down and oil shocks fade, the direction of crude between now and the end of 2027 will be lower, spurred by a big consumer shift.

Daan Struyven, co-head of global commodities research at Goldman Sachs and a team of analysts, wrote that they see the war-driven oil shocks leading to a surge in demand for electric vehicles, ultimately putting downward pressure on oil prices.

"We estimate a potential hit to global oil demand in December 2027 of 0.13-0.32mb/d (or 0.1-0.3% of global demand), depending on the persistence of the realized EV car sales acceleration," Struyven wrote.

EV sales had declined significantly recently, sparking speculation that the elimination of federal tax incentives would continue to crush the market. But as Goldman highlighted in the note, EVs have come roaring back globally in response to the surge in gas prices caused by the Iran war.

What smart people are saying about oil prices
©Brandon Bell/Getty Images
The Iran war sent oil up — then down — in volatile trade, rattling markets and stoking inflation fears.
Gulf producers have cut output amid shipping constraints, raising recession concerns.
Analysts warn the rally could echo a 1970s-style oil shock.
Oil futures have swung sharply in rollercoaster trading this week, as investors react to the risk of a prolonged disruption to crude shipments through the Strait of Hormuz.

Brent and West Texas Intermediate crude futures breached the key $100-a-barrel level after trade opened on Sunday evening and have since fallen sharply after President Donald Trump suggested the Iran war could be over soon.

Traffic through the critical oil chokepoint remains constrained a week after the US and Israel attacked Iran, while several major Gulf producers have begun slowing output.

Oil prices have nearly doubled in just over three months in 2026, raising fresh concerns about inflation and the outlook for the economy and stock market.

Here's what energy, business, and finance experts are saying about oil's break above the key $100 a barrel psychological barrier.

Oil just got repriced by diplomacy, not drilling.

Brent fell below $90 after Trump said an Iran peace deal was close.

Goldman also reset its 2027 oil forecast.

The market is trading one question:

Does geopolitical risk come out of barrels next?

Hey @grok is there record oil flowing through the Strait of Hormuz?

No, there are no records of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz right now—flows remain well below pre-conflict levels (around 20 million barrels per day of crude and products), though they have rebounded somewhat in June 2026 amid a fragile ceasefire.05
Pre-Conflict Baseline
•In 2025 and early 2026 (pre-late February conflict), the strait typically carried ~20–21 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude oil, condensate, and petroleum products, plus significant LNG. This represented about 20–25% of global seaborne oil trade.18
•EIA data for Q4 2025 showed ~20.7 million bpd; flows dropped sharply after the US-Israeli strikes on Iran began around Feb. 28, 2026.35
Recent Situation (as of mid-June 2026)
•Visible/AIS-tracked flows have been much lower for months due to blockades, tensions, “dark” (AIS-off) transits, and workarounds like pipelines (e.g., Saudi East-West to Yanbu, UAE to Fujairah). Estimates for May/early June: often 1–3 million bpd tracked, with clandestine/“ghost” flows pushing totals higher but still far below normal.2
•Non-Iranian flows rose ~50% in early June to at least 1.8 million bpd (from ~1.2 million in May), per Vortexa data—figures often revised upward with satellite analysis.8
•US officials (e.g., Energy Secretary Chris Wright) claimed ~7 million bpd in mid-June (roughly half the pre-war gap, and rising), aided by naval escorts and a reported “secret project.” Independent trackers (Kpler, Vortexa, JPMorgan) generally show lower verifiable numbers and dispute the highest claims.5
•Post-ceasefire (around mid-June), there have been spikes: e.g., claims of 12+ million barrels in one night/day (a high since the conflict began, with Saudi supertankers and others moving), plus increased traffic as confidence returns. Stranded oil is flowing out, but full recovery is expected to take weeks/months.13
•Iranian exports were heavily restricted earlier but have shown signs of resuming.
Context on “Records”
•No sources report flows exceeding or even matching the pre-war ~20 million bpd average. Recent activity reflects partial recovery and catch-up of stranded cargoes after disruption (described as the largest oil supply shock in history, with cumulative Gulf losses in the hundreds of millions to over 1 billion barrels).3
•Data is uncertain due to dark fleets, spoofing, and conflicting claims from US/Iran sides. Independent firms like Kpler, Vortexa, and EIA emphasize revisions and undercounting.7
In short, oil is flowing again and increasing (especially non-Iranian and post-deal), but it is not at record levels—it’s a partial rebound from severe wartime disruption. Markets have adapted somewhat via bypasses and inventories, keeping prices from spiking as much as initially feared. For the absolute latest, check sources like EIA, Kpler, or Vortexa, as the situation evolves quickly.

Trump says Iran agreed to inspections, Tehran denies new nuclear commitment

Summary

• US President Donald Trump said Iran had agreed to long-term, high-level nuclear inspections, adding that he would not allow further negotiations without such an agreement.

• Trump said funds and sanctions relief being released by the US Treasury would go into a US-controlled escrow account and be used only to buy food and medical supplies from the United States, including corn, wheat, and soybeans.

• Iran's ambassador to the United Nations in Geneva said on Tuesday that Lebanon was an "unquestionable" part of the memorandum of understanding with the US and that it included the withdrawal of Israeli troops.

• The Israeli military said it struck armed militants in southern Lebanon on Tuesday after identifying a cell operating close to Israeli soldiers in the Ali al-Taher Ridge area.

• Iran has no plan to allow IAEA inspectors to visit nuclear sites targeted during the recent conflict, Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei said on Tuesday.

• Israeli Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich said on Tuesday that Israel would not withdraw its forces from southern Lebanon as long as Iran-backed Hezbollah remained active there and the current Israeli government stayed in power.

• Iran-backed Lebanese group Hezbollah said on Tuesday that an Israeli attack in southern Lebanon violated the ceasefire agreement, adding that Israeli forces had fired at a group of civilians.

• Israeli Prime Minister BenjaminNetanyahu said Israel's confrontation with Iran and its allies was "not over," during a meeting last Thursday with reserve officer cadets in Gush Etzion, Israeli media reported on Tuesday.

• The head of the Iranian army's center for studies and research said some Muslim Arab countries in the region helped create the conditions for the 12-day war by providing financial support to Israel and the United States.

• Iran and Oman formed a joint committee to hold talks on the Strait of Hormuz after an Iranian delegation's visit to Muscat, Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf said on Tuesday.

55 minutes ago

Ex-PM says Israel smuggled Starlink systems into Iran

Former Israeli Prime Minister Naftali Bennett said on Tuesday that Israel smuggled Starlink internet receivers into Iran to help anti-government protesters maintain internet access.

Speaking at the JNS International Policy Summit in Jerusalem, Bennett said he had launched a plan to acquire and covertly transfer "tens of thousands" of Starlink receivers into Iran.

However, Bennett said the effort was not fully pursued after he left office, criticizing Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's government for failing to follow through on the initiative.

58 minutes ago

IRGC-linked media rejects Pakistan PM's missile talks remarks

Fars News Agency, affiliated with Iran's Revolutionary Guards, dismissed remarks by Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif that Iran's ballistic missile program was discussed in negotiations, calling the comments "completely incorrect" and likely the result of misinformation.

Fars also said Pakistan currently plays only a limited role in mediation and suggested that Sharif's remarks were aimed at highlighting Islamabad's diplomatic importance. The agency added that Qatar is currently the most influential mediator between Iran and the United States.

1 hour ago

Iran and US trade rival readings of MoU before 60-day talks mature

The US-Iran memorandum is being implemented before Washington and Tehran have agreed on its meaning, turning the fragile deal into a battle over interpretation across the Strait of Hormuz, frozen funds, nuclear inspections, oil sanctions, and Lebanon.

Less than a week after the two sides signed the MoU to end more than three months of war, its contradictions are already shaping the next phase of diplomacy: Hormuz is open, but ships may still need Iranian permission; funds are "available," but Washington says they may be channeled toward wheat, corn and other approved purchases; inspectors are "back," according to US officials, but Iran says there is no plan for UN inspectors to visit bombed nuclear sites; oil sales have been authorized, but Vice President JD Vance says Tehran will not benefit unless it changes behavior; Lebanon is written into the deal, but Israel is not a party to it.

For Iran, ambiguity has become a source of leverage. Officials in Tehran are insisting that implementation of the MoU's early provisions is a precondition for talks on more sensitive issues, while rejecting US descriptions of what the next stage should include.

--- Iran International English (@IranIntl_En) June 23, 2026

🛢️Oil Markets Brace as Crude Stockpile Estimates Show Continued Draw Down Amid Hormuz Standoff

US crude inventories fell by an estimated 765,000 barrels last week, according to API figures released ahead of Wednesday’s official EIA report.

The estimates land as oil markets stay on edge over the Strait of Hormuz, the chokepoint off Iran’s coast through which around a fifth of the world’s oil passes. Any disruption there reverberates through global prices.

Other API estimates for the week:
🔸 Gasoline: +1.238 million barrels 
🔸 Distillates: +1.447 million barrels 
🔸 Cushing hub (the Oklahoma delivery point for US benchmark crude): -982,000 barrels

🔸 Strategic Petroleum Reserve (official figure, not estimate): -9.1 million barrels, now at its lowest level in more than 40 years

API releases are an industry preview of the government’s official inventory data and can move prices before EIA confirms them.

Brent crude futures, the international benchmark, fell 0.5% overnight. This follows a nearly 1% drop on Tuesday, marking its lowest level since early March.

Several months of consistent crude inventory draws mostly 3-8M barrels weekly clearly signals strong demand vs supply
It's turning from short-term news into a structural bullish trend for oil

Leonidas: UK Crude Market Open Brief
ICE Brent (AUG) is trading ~$76.35/bbl, extending losses for a fourth session and now below the pre-war Feb 27 close. The regime change from disruption premium to oversupply pricing is confirmed. Overnight, Oman announced a temporary IMO-backed maritime corridor through Hormuz; crossings nearly tripled w/w to 93 vessels (June 19–21). Trump confirmed 19m barrels transited in a single day and pledged no further naval blockade. Brent settled $77.08 on Tuesday — lowest since the war began — and shed another ~$0.70 in Asian trade. Hedge fund bearish positioning remains at a five-month high, with net-length already slashed ~95k contracts and shorts still building. WTI settled $73.21, also at four-month lows. API: crude -765k bbl, but gasoline +1.2m and distillates +1.4m suggest demand lagging. SPR fell 9.1m bbl to its lowest in 40+ years. Trump's DOJ gasoline "price gouging" probe adds political pressure.

Structure continues to deteriorate. Brent M1/M2 at $0.20/bbl — barely backwardated, floor of its 60-day range. Dubai M1/M2 in contango at -$0.97/bbl. DFL negative at -$0.42/bbl, the fastest-weakening spread on the board — North Sea physical tightness fully evaporated. The DFL collapse is mechanically resetting Forties' export competitiveness (~$1.80/bbl cheap to basket, NWE arbs open), but the direction is still deteriorating. Critical watch: whether Brent M1/M2 holds above flat — a contango flip would be regime-defining. WTI M1/M2 at $0.61/bbl is the last benchmark with meaningful backwardation, also at 60-day lows.

Supporting the North American session, a widening US Current Account deficit and softer housing data will test dollar resilience.

Wednesday June 24 2026 | Overnight Action

Saturday week-ahead notes flagged the 7350 to 7300 zone as the deep destination if the post-OPEX unwind fired. Today it did. Tuesday closed 7365.46, down 107.33 points, almost all of it an overnight gap. VIX jumped to 19.49 but most of that rise was mechanical, not new fear. The map reset lower. 7400 is the magnet that pins the session and 7350 is the line that decides the downside. Micron reports after-hours Wednesday. That print moves Thursday open alongside Core PCE at 8:30 AM, not Wednesday's session.

THE NUMBERS

SPX Close: 7365.46 (Tuesday −107.33, −1.44%)
VIX: 19.49 | VIX9D: 19.47 | VVIX: 99.5
Daily 1SD: +/- 90.3 (7275 to 7456)
Weekly 1SD: +/- 199.1 (7166 to 7565)
ATR 5-day: +/- 125.2 pts | ATR 14-day: +/- 125.2 pts
Dealer: SELL PRESSURE (rallies get sold near spot, resistance on the way up)
20D EMA: 7441.96 | 20D SMA: 7481.61 | 50D EMA: 7315.42 | 50D SMA: 7339.73

EM Straddle Term Structure (ref 7365, ATM to spot):
Wed 6/24: ±60.85 (7305 to 7426) MU after market close
Thu 6/25: ±88.30 (7277 to 7453) PCE Day
Fri 6/26: ±108.20 (7257 to 7473) Quarter End

ATM IV ramp (7365 strike):
Wed 20.95% · Thu 20.62% · Fri 20.39%

ECONOMIC EVENTS

Wednesday 6/24. MBA Mortgage data 7:00 AM. Current Account Q1 and Building Permits Final May 8:30 AM. New Home Sales May 10:00 AM. EIA Crude Stocks 10:30 AM. 17-Week Bill and 2-Year FRN Auctions 11:30 AM. 5-Year Note Auction 1:00 PM. Fed Bank Stress Test Results 4:00 PM. Earnings: Micron after-hours 10.95 percent implied move.

Thursday 6/25. Core PCE Price Index 8:30 AM. GDP Q1 Final 8:30 AM. Durable Goods 8:30 AM. Personal Income and Spending 8:30 AM. Initial and Continuing Jobless Claims 8:30 AM. Kansas Fed indices 11:00 AM. Fed Williams 3:40 PM. Fed Goolsbee 6:30 PM.

Friday 6/26. Goods Trade Balance 8:30 AM. Michigan Sentiment Final and inflation expectations 10:00 AM. Fed Williams 10:30 AM. Fed Kashkari 11:30 AM. Last full trading day of Q2.

THE VOL PICTURE

VIX rose 2.21 points Tuesday from 17.28 to 19.49, a 12.79 percent jump. The internals tell a softer story than the headline.

Sticky Strike was the dominant factor at 1.34, more than half the move. Parallel Shift came in at 0.51. Call Skew 0.18 and Upside Convexity 0.24 were both positive. Put Skew was only 0.06 and Downside Convexity actually fell 0.12. Total VIX change 2.21.

What that means in plain terms. Most of Tuesday's VIX rise was mechanical. Price fell and slid into a higher part of the curve, so the fixed strike reading went up on its own. That is the Sticky Strike piece. The genuine new protection bid, the Parallel Shift, was much smaller. There was no rush into downside protection. Put skew barely moved and downside convexity dropped. If anything traders paid up slightly for upside. This is the opposite of Monday, when the rise was real protection bidding. Tuesday's vol rise is more fragile. A push back toward 7400 and 7425 deflates a good chunk of it fast.

VVIX jumped to 99.5 from 91.72 and is sitting just under 100. The vol of vol is bid ahead of PCE Thursday even though spot vol itself looks more mechanical than fearful.

The implied vol picture is front loaded. ATM at 7365 prints 20.95 percent for Wednesday, 20.62 percent for Thursday PCE, and 20.39 percent for Friday Quarter End. Percentage vol is richest at the front because the move is freshest there. The dollar straddle still widens with time. Wednesday prices ±60.85, Thursday ±88.30, Friday ±108.20. In basis points of the index that is roughly 83 bps Wednesday, 120 bps Thursday, and 147 bps Friday. Position to the dollar straddle. The market is pricing the most uncertainty for the immediate session while the dollar range stretches into PCE and quarter end.

WHAT HAPPENED TUESDAY

SPX closed 7365.46, down 107.33 points or 1.44 percent. The drop was the overnight gap. Price opened 7366.51 and went nowhere from there. It ranged from 7347.60 to 7424.17 and closed near the open. The high stalled under 7425. The low held 7350.

AI mega caps stayed under pressure. Money kept rotating toward defensives while traders paid up for protection into PCE Thursday. Micron reports Wednesday after-hours with a 10.95 percent implied move. Micron is roughly 1.8 percent of SPX, 5.1 percent of Nasdaq, and 5.6 percent of SMH, so the print drives Thursday open, not Wednesday's session.

WHAT THIS MEANS FOR WEDNESDAY

The map reset lower and it is cleaner than it looks. 7400 carries the strongest support on the map. Price gets pulled up and held there. That is the magnet for the session. The 7375 to 7400 band is a support shelf under it. Below that the floor is 7350, the heaviest line near spot, where heavy resistance sits on top of heavy downside fuel. That mix held the low Tuesday at 7347.60. Hold 7350 and price pins toward 7400. Lose it and the downside opens fast.

Wednesday reads calm and dampened. The day flags long gamma, which means moves get absorbed and price tends to coil around the 7400 magnet while traders wait for Thursday. The dealer read flipped to sell pressure. Rallies into 7425 get sold instead of bought. That is a change from Monday.

Above spot the path runs 7375 and 7385 support, then 7400 where price wants to settle. 7425 is the break level where Tuesday's high stalled. Calls are quiet inside the whole daily range. The first real overhead wall does not show up until 7500. So if 7425 breaks, the runway up is thin, with fuel up near 7475 toward 7500.

Below spot the first catch is 7360. Then 7350, the line that decides the downside. Lose 7350 and 7340 the 50D SMA, then 7330 the next catch. Below 7330 the fuel down stacks at 7320 where the 50D EMA sits at 7315, then 7300, into 7275 the 1SD low and the deep level for the week.

Micron reports after-hours so Wednesday is positioning only. The number moves Thursday open with Core PCE at 8:30 AM. Do not trade Wednesday's session around the Micron print.

Bottom line. Tuesday was an overnight gap, not a session flush, and most of the VIX rise was mechanical. Wednesday coils around the 7400 magnet inside 7350 to 7425 while the market waits on PCE. Break 7425 and the path up is thin to 7455 then toward 7500. Lose 7350 and the fuel down opens to 7320, then 7300, then 7275.

THE LEVELS

EM Straddle envelope is 7305 to 7426. Daily 1SD is 7275 to 7456.

⚡ 7425   break level both ways · EM straddle top 7426 · high stalled here · accelerates either way

IF THEN

   First 7385 support then 7400 the magnet where price wants to settle
   Through it the next test is 7425 the break level. Calls are quiet above it so the runway is thin
   Above 7425 the first stop is 7455 at the 1SD top, then fuel up near 7475 toward the 7500 wall
   This is the long gamma coil higher scenario. The mechanical part of the VIX rise deflates as price recovers.

⏸ IF SPX trades between 7350 and 7425
   Coil day pinned to the 7400 magnet while the market waits for PCE Thursday
   The EM straddle expects ±61 pts. Trade range bounces small. Stay light until 7425 breaks or 7350 cracks.

🔽 IF SPX loses 7350
   THEN 7340 the 50D SMA, then 7330 the next catch
   Below 7330 the fuel down opens to 7320 where the 50D EMA sits at 7315, then 7300
   This is the continuation scenario where vol keeps building into PCE.

🔽 IF SPX loses 7300 and 7275
   THEN price is below the 1SD low with little to slow it until 7250
   Below 7250 opens the deeper bear extension near 7240 and lower
   The hawkish tail. Reserve it for a hot rates read or a fresh AI mega cap leg lower.

NEXT DAY PREVIEW

Thursday 6/25. CAUTION flag. The big one. Core PCE Price Index at 8:30 AM with GDP Q1 Final, Durable Goods, Personal Income and Spending, and Jobless Claims all at 8:30 AM. Fed Williams 3:40 PM and Goolsbee 6:30 PM. Micron's after-hours print from Wednesday moves the open. Thursday EM straddle ±88.30 ref 7365 (7277 to 7453). PCE at 8:30 sets the day before the bell. Two catalysts hit the open at once. Trade smaller. Vol expansion ahead.

RULES

1. Tuesday was an overnight gap, not a session flush. The cash session traded both sides between 7347 and 7424 and closed flat near the open. Trade the new map off 7365.
2. 7400 carries the strongest support on the map. It is the magnet that pins the session. Price gets pulled up and held there.
3. 7425 is the break level where Tuesday's high stalled. Calls are quiet above it so a break runs thin toward 7455 then 7500.
4. 7350 is the floor and the downside trigger. Heavy resistance on top of heavy downside fuel. It held the low Tuesday. Lose it and price drops fast.
5. Below 7350 the 50D SMA sits at 7339.73, then 7330 is the catch. Below that the fuel down stacks at 7320, the 50D EMA at 7315, then 7300.
6. 7275 is the 1SD low and the deep level for the week. 7250 is the catch under it.
7. Dealer flipped to sell pressure. Rallies get sold near spot now. That is a change from Monday.
8. Most of Tuesday's VIX rise was mechanical. Sticky Strike led, not real protection bidding. A bounce deflates it fast.
9. Front loaded vol means percentage IV is richest today. The dollar straddle still widens into PCE. Size to the dollar move.
10. Micron reports after-hours. Wednesday is positioning only. The print drives Thursday open with PCE.

Oil tanker booked in Persian Gulf at 897% of benchmark rate https://t.co/8Ibyx7xwGb

Oil Tanker Booked in Gulf at 897% of Benchmark Freight Rate

 A supertanker has been provisionally booked to transport oil from the Persian Gulf to India at a rate equivalent to almost nine times benchmark freight costs, an eye-watering price that reflects the shortage of available, empty vessels in the area.

The very-large crude carrier, capable of handling about two million barrels of oil, will be supplied by South Korea shipowner Sinokor at 897 Worldscale points, or 897% of the benchmark, according to shipbrokers. That fee ranks as the highest so far this year.

A message from the company received by shipbrokers on Wednesday and seen by Bloomberg News offered VLCCs for the loading of oil from Iraq’s Basrah terminal by June 24. It indicated it would pass through the Strait of Hormuz with the cargo. (Bloomberg)

🛢️ OPEC's 2050 outlook has a message the transition debate keeps missing. Every fuel grows except coal.

OPEC's World Oil Outlook 2026 revised global oil demand higher, citing new energy and industrial policies.

The fuel chart is the tell. 
Solar adds the most new demand through 2050.

But oil and gas still grow 17-19 mboe/d each. 
They don't shrink.

Coal is the only fuel that falls, dropping sharply after 2030.

This is an "all of the above" world, not renewables replacing hydrocarbons.

The regional split is just as stark. 

India leads at 2.8% a year, the fastest on the board.

Meanwhile China (-0.1%), Russia (-0.3%), and OECD Europe (~0.0%) flatline or shrink.

OPEC says $17.7 trillion of investment is needed to sustain the system.

security and transition have to advance together. 

The challenge isn't just cutting emissions, it's keeping energy reliable and affordable.

Watch where that $17.7 tn flows. 

If it underfunds oil and gas, the supply gap shows up well before 2050.

#BrentCrude #WTI #OilMarkets #Commodities #Energy #Macro #Inflation

EIA's 2025 data shows OPEC+ producing 39 mb/d combined. 

OPEC at 24.6, non-OPEC partners at 14.3.

Saudi Arabia leads at 9.3 mb/d, with Russia just behind at 9.1.

But UAE left OPEC in May 2026.

That removes 3.4 mb/d, a producer the size of Iran, from the group's quota system.

The UAE has long chafed at production caps that hold back its expanding capacity.

Its exit means one less barrel under OPEC's control, and one more producer pumping to its own plan.

This is how cartels lose pricing power. 

Not in one collapse, but one defection at a time.

If the UAE pumps freely, the pressure on others to follow grows.
